---
title: "Project Theseus as Report-First Implementation Reference"
chapter_id: "project-theseus-as-report-first-implementation-reference"
part_id: "evidence-implementation-living-book"
status: "conceptual"
draft_maturity: "v0.2 manuscript draft"
last_updated: "2026-06-25"
primary_sources:
  - "project_theseus_whitepaper"
  - "theseus_plan_compiler"
  - "theseus_self_evolution_system"
  - "theseus_architecture_gate"
  - "theseus_operator_os"
  - "theseus_circle_transfer"
evidence_level: "argument"
claim_label: "Design rationale"
open_evidence_gaps:
  - "Exact source-note mappings now cover all assigned sources for this core claim; support remains argument until current Theseus reports, replay commands, benchmark environments, or accepted evidence transitions justify promotion."
  - "The theseus_report_crosswalk_record fixture validates schema/example consistency only; no live Theseus report bundle, replay command, benchmark environment, or current gate output has been imported or rerun."
  - "No support-state promotion is implied by this drafting pass."
---

# Project Theseus as Report-First Implementation Reference

## Chapter status

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Chapter ID | `project-theseus-as-report-first-implementation-reference` |
| Part | Part IV - Evidence, Implementation, and the Living Book |
| Status | conceptual |
| Manuscript maturity | v0.2 manuscript draft |
| Last updated | 2026-06-25 |
| Primary source records | `project_theseus_whitepaper`, `theseus_plan_compiler`, `theseus_self_evolution_system`, `theseus_architecture_gate`, `theseus_operator_os`, `theseus_circle_transfer` |
| Claim label | Design rationale |
| Evidence level | argument |
| Source queue | primary: `project_theseus_whitepaper`, `theseus_plan_compiler`, `theseus_self_evolution_system`; supporting: `theseus_architecture_gate`, `theseus_operator_os`, `theseus_circle_transfer`, `rmi`, `cgs`, `benchmaxxing`, `cognitive_loop_closure`, `viea`, `vcm_public`, `scf` |
| Source loading state | source notes: `project_theseus_whitepaper`, `theseus_plan_compiler`, `theseus_self_evolution_system`, `theseus_architecture_gate`, `theseus_operator_os`, `theseus_circle_transfer` |
| Test state | `theseus_report_crosswalk_record.valid.json` passes repository-level protocol fixture validation; `AsiStackProofs.TheseusReference` implements finite implementation-reference artifact-surface and gate-before-promotion predicates; report crosswalk, architecture-gate mapping, work-board, and self-evolution ladder audits remain planned. |

## Drafting guardrail

This chapter uses Project Theseus sources as report-first implementation references. It does not import private project artifacts, rerun benchmarks, verify current dashboards, or promote source-reported implementation state into public empirical evidence.

## Problem

The book needs a concrete implementation-reference chapter for how the ASI stack can be operated as report-first local machinery instead of only conceptual layer diagrams. Project Theseus is useful because its source notes describe pressure loops, plan compiler contracts, architecture gates, work boards, residual escrow, and self-evolution controls as reportable machinery.

The boundary is just as important as the reference. A report-first architecture can guide this book, but the public repo cannot claim current Theseus benchmark performance, training readiness, or self-evolution safety unless the relevant reports, commands, environment notes, and publication permissions are present here.

## Why existing approaches are insufficient

A prototype roadmap is not enough unless readers can see how goals, plans, arms, reports, gates, residuals, checkpoints, compute, and operator surfaces interact in a working research system. Dashboards and summaries are especially risky because they can hide the run record that should carry the claim.

This chapter therefore treats reports, ledgers, configs, and work-board entries as the authority surface. Dashboard prose is a view over those artifacts, not a substitute for them.

## Core Claim

[project-theseus-as-report-first-implementation-reference.core, label: Design rationale, support: argument] Project Theseus should be mined as a report-first implementation reference for RMI, showing how pressure, residuals, gates, ledgers, operator work boards, checkpoints, and self-evolution governance can instantiate the ASI stack without promoting local results as general capability claims.

The claim remains at `argument` support. The source notes support report-first implementation discussion, but they do not give this repository reproduced benchmark runs, current gate outputs, or public-safe private workload artifacts.

## Mechanism

Theseus is useful here because it makes the stack operationally visible. The whitepaper gives the pressure loop: benchmark or operator demand, attempted work, residual, diagnosis, compression, verification, new structure, and renewed pressure. The plan compiler gives that loop a contract surface through typed goals, semantic IR DAGs, VCM slices, executor routes, claim/evidence targets, and replay traces. The self-evolution and architecture-gate notes keep the loop from becoming open-ended mutation: architecture changes, teacher edits, training runs, and candidate promotion require reports, branches, checks, ledgers, and gates.

The operator OS adds the human-control surface. Work does not disappear into a dashboard; it becomes board state, command vocabulary, node eligibility, hook output, feedback route, and safety-visible control. The Circle transfer lane adds a second boundary: deterministic structural fixtures can shape private experiments, but they cannot become model-quality evidence without named workloads, baselines, metrics, scripts, and reports.

The implementation-reference view is two coupled loops: the Theseus report loop that turns pressure into governed structure, and the Circle transfer lane that can supply deterministic contract fixtures without becoming model-quality evidence.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  Goal["Goal, benchmark, or operator task"] --> Plan["Plan compiler contract and semantic IR"]
  Plan --> Route["Octopus router and bounded arms"]
  Route --> Work["SymLiquid, tools, Hive task kinds"]
  Work --> Reports["Reports, ledgers, traces, work board"]
  Reports --> Gate["Architecture, resource, promotion, and safety gates"]
  Gate --> Residual["Residual escrow and intervention ladder"]
  Residual --> Structure["Tool, adapter, checkpoint, or smallest source change"]
  Structure --> Goal

  Circle["Circle finite contract pack"] --> Transfer["Theseus Circle transfer lane"]
  Transfer --> Fixtures["Private workload design and structural smokes"]
  Fixtures --> Boundary["Claim-boundary ledger"]
  Boundary --> Gate
  Boundary -. blocks .-> Overclaim["Quality, speed, context, or ASI claims without workload evidence"]
```

Operationally, the layer should produce artifacts that can be inspected without rerunning the whole cognition path. A downstream reader should be able to distinguish source-note lineage, imported reports, reproduced runs, missing artifacts, and public non-claims.

## Interfaces

- The plan compiler supplies contracts, semantic IR DAGs, VCM slices, routes, claim targets, and replay traces.
- Octopus arms, SymLiquid CGS, SparkStream control, and Hive runtime map stack layers into reports and configs.
- Genesis-style artifact kernels preserve claims, critiques, benchmark results, tool promotions, architecture decisions, and feedback.
- Capability matrices and benchmark ledgers expose readiness and residuals without turning local reports into public empirical proof.

The interface is the Theseus Report Crosswalk Record: stack layer, source reports, config/tool refs, gate refs, residual refs, public claim boundary, verification status, missing artifacts, non-claims, and evidence refs.

## Invariants

The Theseus invariants preserve the boundary between report-first implementation evidence and public-book claims: a dashboard, work board, or private run can orient the architecture only when its report trail survives.

- The frontier moves while the regression floor holds.
- Promotion uses external-inference-zero evidence where the local gate requires it.
- Reports survive the run and are not replaced by dashboard prose.
- Private experiments and public calibration remain separated.
- Unknown task kinds are blocked rather than guessed.
- Architecture changes follow the intervention ladder.

The strongest invariant is report primacy: if a claim relies on a run, gate, benchmark, or work-board state, the report or replayable artifact must exist before the claim can move.

## Failure modes

- Latest-file report overwrites history.
- Benchmark overfit becomes capability evidence.
- Teacher dependence replaces local evidence.
- Architecture churn outruns residual diagnosis.
- Remote work proceeds without allowlists, TTLs, or kill switches.
- Local prototype results are overclaimed as public evidence.

Each failure should produce a residual, blocked promotion, or missing-artifact row. The public book should prefer saying "not imported or not rerun" over smoothing source-reported implementation state into a stronger claim.

## Minimal implementation

The smallest useful implementation is a Theseus report crosswalk record from ASI Stack layer to source reports, configs, gates, residuals, public claim boundary, verification status, and missing artifacts.

The current fixture validates only that public-safe crosswalk shape. It does not import a current Theseus report bundle or reproduce a benchmark.

## Codex test plan

| Test | Purpose | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Theseus report crosswalk fixture validation | Validate that a report-first reference row names source reports, config/tool refs, gates, residuals, public claim boundary, verification status, missing artifacts, non-claims, and evidence refs. | implemented; passing via `python3 scripts/validate_protocol_examples.py` |
| Theseus report crosswalk completeness test | Import a public-safe Theseus report bundle and verify that every cited report, config, gate, residual, and missing artifact maps to a stack layer. | planned; not run |
| Architecture gate mapping test | Check that each Theseus gate maps to an ASI Stack promotion, rollback, quarantine, or residual-retention decision. | planned; not run |
| Work-board improvement contract test | Check that a work-board item declares objective, gate, evidence artifact, residual, owner, and completion criterion before being treated as progress. | planned; not run |
| Self-evolution intervention ladder audit | Check that proposed self-evolution interventions are ordered by risk and authority and cannot skip the required review level. | planned; not run |

The implemented row validates fixture/schema consistency only. The remaining rows require imported or reproduced Theseus report artifacts; they are not reported benchmark or readiness results. When a Theseus audit is implemented, this chapter should link to the command, fixture, report bundle, environment notes, and result summary, and Appendix E should be regenerated or updated accordingly.

### Formalization hooks

| Tag | Module | Target | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| `lean:theseus.reference.report_contract.operational_invariant` | `AsiStackProofs.TheseusReference` | An implementation-reference claim names the report, config, or tool surface and does not rely on dashboard prose alone. | implemented |
| `lean:theseus.reference.gate_before_promotion.failure_blocks_promotion` | `AsiStackProofs.TheseusReference` | A capability or self-evolution promotion is blocked when required gate reports are absent or failing. | implemented |

These Lean hooks are implemented as finite-record predicates over implementation-reference claim reviews and gate-before-promotion reviews. They prove only the record-level gates: implementation-reference claims require a report, config, or tool surface rather than dashboard prose alone, and capability or self-evolution promotion is blocked without present passing gate reports. They do not prove any Theseus gate passed, import private Theseus reports, rerun benchmarks, verify dashboard state, or establish self-evolution safety.

## Source crosswalk

| Source ID | Title | Layer | Planned use | Readiness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `project_theseus_whitepaper` | Project Theseus Whitepaper | report_first_rmi_prototype | Local-first report-driven RMI implementation reference: SymLiquid, SparkStream, Octopus Router, residual escrow, self-evolution gates, Hive runtime, observability. | source note available |
| `theseus_plan_compiler` | Theseus Plan Compiler | planning_control | Goal-to-contract compiler with semantic IR DAGs, VCM context slices, executor routes, claim/evidence targets, contract hashes, and replay traces. | source note available |
| `theseus_self_evolution_system` | Theseus Self-Evolution System | recursive_self_improvement_governance | Evidence-first self-evolution lane with intervention ladder, ATTD repo-health gate, guarded teacher self-edit, architecture experiment governance, loop closure, and outcome ledger. | source note available |
| `theseus_architecture_gate` | Theseus Architecture Gate | readiness_gate_governance | Pre-training readiness gate covering ratchet completeness, router readiness, safety ledger, residual escrow, bridge benchmarks, procedural tools, routing memory, lifecycle governance, and external-inference zero. | source note available |
| `theseus_operator_os` | Hive Operator OS and Work Board | labor_os_operator_surface | Shared command vocabulary, durable SQLite work board, node registry, background/watch/wake contracts, skill registry, tool hooks, feedback routing, and safety-visible operator surface. | source note available |
| `theseus_circle_transfer` | Theseus Circle Calculus Transfer Lane | proof_contract_transfer | Report-only bridge from Circle finite fixtures into private Theseus benchmark design with explicit quality/runtime/memory/transfer/failure-case claim boundaries. | source note available |

The crosswalk keeps the reference public-safe. The whitepaper supplies the RMI/report-first frame, the plan compiler supplies goal-to-contract machinery, the self-evolution source supplies intervention-gate discipline, the architecture gate supplies readiness boundaries, the operator OS supplies work-board and node-control surfaces, and the Circle transfer source supplies structural fixture non-claims. None of those source notes is a reproduced public benchmark run.

## Summary

Project Theseus gives the book a concrete report-first implementation reference: pressure, residuals, plan contracts, gates, ledgers, operator work boards, checkpoints, and self-evolution governance.

The public boundary is strict. Until current reports, commands, environments, and permissioned artifacts are imported or reproduced, Theseus remains a design/reference source for the ASI Stack rather than public empirical proof of capability. The next chapter converts that caution into a build order: evidence infrastructure first, capability growth second, self-improvement last.
